name: codex
description: Use when the user asks to run Codex CLI (codex exec, codex resume) or references OpenAI Codex for code analysis, refactoring, or automated editing. Uses GPT-5.2 by default for state-of-the-art software engineering.
capabilities: Invokes OpenAI Codex CLI (codex exec) for code analysis and refactoring tasks
Supports multiple GPT-5.2 model variants including gpt-5.2-max, gpt-5.2, and gpt-5.2-mini
Configures sandbox modes (read-only, workspace-write, danger-full-access) for controlled execution
Manages reasoning effort levels (xhigh, high, medium, low) for different task complexities
Enables session resumption with context preservation across Codex invocations
Filters thinking tokens to manage context window usage during long-running tasks
author: softaworks
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill (README.md + SKILL.md). Static analyzer flagged patterns in documentation examples as potential security issues, but all findings are false positives. No executable scripts exist. The skill is a prompt wrapper that provides Claude with context for invoking the OpenAI Codex CLI tool.
valueStatement: Complex code analysis and refactoring tasks require specialized AI models with advanced reasoning capabilities. This skill enables Claude Code to invoke OpenAI Codex CLI, providing access to GPT-5.2 with 76.3% SWE-bench performance for deep code understanding, automated refactoring, and comprehensive software engineering workflows.
userTitle: Run Codex CLI for Code Analysis and Refactoring
limitations: Requires Codex CLI v0.57.0 or later installed and configured on the system
Does not include Codex CLI; users must install and configure credentials separately
Cannot execute code directly; Claude invokes the external codex CLI based on skill guidelines
Network access depends on sandbox mode configuration; read-only is the default

useCases.targetUser: Senior developers and engineering leads reviewing legacy codebases
useCases.title: Comprehensive Codebase Analysis
useCases.description: Analyze entire repositories to identify architectural issues, code smells, security vulnerabilities, and optimization opportunities using Codex advanced reasoning capabilities.

useCases.targetUser: Developers modernizing codebases or addressing technical debt
useCases.title: Automated Refactoring Campaigns
useCases.description: Execute multi-file refactoring tasks with appropriate sandbox permissions, from simple function extractions to complex architectural patterns.

useCases.targetUser: AI-assisted development teams building complex features
useCases.title: Session-Based Iterative Development
useCases.description: Start a Codex session, make incremental changes, and resume with preserved context for complex multi-step software engineering tasks.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Code Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Use codex to analyze this codebase and identify areas for improvement. Use high reasoning effort and read-only sandbox mode.

promptTemplates.title: Targeted Refactoring
promptTemplates.prompt: Use codex to refactor the authentication module. Use workspace-write sandbox and medium reasoning effort. Make the code more maintainable while preserving functionality.
promptTemplates.scenario: Refactoring a specific module with file modifications

promptTemplates.title: Session-Based Development
promptTemplates.prompt: Start a Codex session to build a new REST API endpoint. Use full-auto mode with danger-full-access sandbox. After completion, tell me how to resume this session for follow-up tasks.
promptTemplates.scenario: Creating new functionality with session continuity

promptTemplates.title: Iterative Improvement
promptTemplates.prompt: Resume the Codex session and continue improving the API implementation. Add input validation and error handling. Use the same settings as before.

bestPractices: Start with read-only analysis before requesting file modifications to understand the codebase structure first
Use appropriate reasoning effort levels—high for architectural changes, low for simple fixes
Explicitly request session resumption when planning multi-step tasks to preserve context
antiPatterns: Asking Codex to make changes across the codebase without specifying a focused scope
Using danger-full-access sandbox for read-only analysis tasks
Starting a new session instead of resuming when continuing previous work

faq.question: How do I install and configure the Codex CLI?
faq.answer: Install the Codex CLI following OpenAI's documentation. Verify installation with 'codex --version' and configure credentials. The skill will invoke codex exec commands based on your prompts.

faq.question: What is the difference between sandbox modes?
faq.answer: Read-only limits access to viewing files. Workspace-write permits creating and modifying files in a sandbox. Danger-full-access allows broad system access. Use read-only for analysis by default.

faq.question: How do I resume a Codex session?
faq.answer: Simply say 'codex resume' or ask to continue with the previous session. Claude will use 'codex exec resume --last' with the same model and settings from the original session.

faq.question: What reasoning effort should I use?
faq.answer: Xhigh for ultra-complex problems requiring deep analysis. High for refactoring, security reviews, and performance optimization. Medium for standard coding tasks. Low for quick fixes and formatting.

faq.question: How does the skill handle thinking tokens?
faq.answer: By default, thinking tokens (stderr) are suppressed to save context window space. Ask to see thinking tokens if you need visibility into Codex's internal reasoning process.

faq.question: Can I switch models mid-session?
faq.answer: Within a Codex session, use the /model slash command to switch models. Between sessions, specify the model in your prompt. The skill defaults to gpt-5.2.
